correctReason: Capturing decision rationale and adaptable implementation guidance lets other teams make the right choices in new contexts, which a bare build narrative cannot provide.

whyFail.B: A verbatim step narrative without rationale does not transfer decision-making to new contexts.
whyFail.C: Config files alone omit the reasoning needed to adapt the approach.
whyFail.D: A tool list without reasoning gives no basis for choosing differently elsewhere.

scenario: A team adopts Claude Code but each developer has divergent local instructions, so generated code violates shared standards inconsistently. Standards live in individual developers' personal user-level config, not in the repo.

constraints.label: Consistency
constraints.text: All developers must get the same standards.

constraints.label: Sharing
constraints.text: Standards must travel with the repository.

constraints.label: Onboarding
constraints.text: New members should inherit standards automatically.

question: Where should the shared standards live for consistent team application?

options.text: In project-level CLAUDE.md (and project-scoped rules/commands) checked into the repository so every developer inherits them on clone/pull.
In each developer's user-level ~/.claude configuration.
In a shared wiki page developers are asked to read.
Pasted manually into each prompt by each developer.

correctReason: Project-level CLAUDE.md and project-scoped configuration checked into the repo are shared via version control, so every developer (including new hires) automatically inherits the same standards.

whyFail.B: User-level config is personal and not shared with teammates via version control, which is the current problem.
whyFail.C: A wiki relies on humans remembering to apply standards; it does not configure the tool.
whyFail.D: Manual pasting is inconsistent and unscalable, the exact failure being observed.

scenario: A team wants a reusable /review command running their code-review checklist, available to every developer automatically when they clone the repository.

constraints.label: Availability
constraints.text: Must be present for all developers on clone/pull.

constraints.label: Sharing
constraints.text: Version-controlled, not personal.

constraints.label: Reuse
constraints.text: Same checklist for the whole team.

question: Where should the command be defined?

options.text: In the project's .claude/commands/ directory, version-controlled in the repository.
In each developer's ~/.claude/commands/ personal directory.
In the root CLAUDE.md as prose instructions.
In a non-existent .claude/config.json commands array.

correctReason: Project-scoped slash commands in .claude/commands/ are version-controlled and automatically available to everyone who clones or pulls the repo.

whyFail.B: Personal ~/.claude/commands/ is not shared via version control.
whyFail.C: CLAUDE.md holds context/instructions, not slash-command definitions.
whyFail.D: That configuration mechanism does not exist in Claude Code.

scenario: A developer runs a codebase-analysis skill that emits very verbose discovery output, which then clutters the main conversation and crowds out the actual task context.

constraints.label: Context hygiene
constraints.text: Verbose output must not pollute the main session.

constraints.label: Isolation
constraints.text: Discovery should run separately and return a summary.

constraints.label: Productivity
constraints.text: Main task context must be preserved.

question: Which configuration best isolates the verbose skill?

options.text: Run the skill with context: fork (or via an Explore-style subagent) so verbose output stays isolated and only a summary returns to the main session.
Run the skill inline and manually delete the noise afterward.
Increase the context window so the noise fits alongside the task.
Disable the skill and do the analysis by hand each time.

correctReason: context: fork (or an Explore subagent) isolates verbose skill output in a separate context and returns only a summary, preserving the main conversation for the task.

whyFail.B: Manual cleanup is error-prone and still pollutes context during the run.
whyFail.C: A bigger window just accommodates the clutter rather than isolating it.
whyFail.D: Disabling a useful skill sacrifices the productivity it provides.

scenario: A developer is debugging a failing function with a clear stack trace pointing to one file; the fix is a single well-understood change. Separately, they must plan a library migration affecting 45+ files with multiple valid approaches.

constraints.label: Task A
constraints.text: Single-file, well-understood bug fix.

constraints.label: Task B
constraints.text: Large, multi-file migration with architectural choices.

constraints.label: Efficiency
constraints.text: Match the mode to the task.

question: Which use of plan mode vs direct execution is correct?

options.text: Use direct execution for the single-file bug fix and plan mode for the 45-file migration with multiple valid approaches.
Use plan mode for both to be thorough.
Use direct execution for both to move fast.
Use plan mode for the bug fix and direct execution for the migration.

correctReason: Direct execution suits the well-scoped single-file fix, while plan mode fits the large, multi-approach migration where exploration and design prevent costly rework, matching mode to complexity.

whyFail.B: Plan mode on a trivial one-line fix is unnecessary overhead.
whyFail.C: Direct execution on a 45-file architectural migration risks late-discovered dependencies and rework.
whyFail.D: This inverts the correct mapping, over-planning the trivial task and under-planning the complex one.

scenario: A team integrating Claude Code into CI finds the job hangs waiting for interactive input, and downstream steps cannot parse the free-text output to post inline PR comments.

constraints.label: Non-interactive
constraints.text: CI must not wait for interactive input.

constraints.label: Machine-readable
constraints.text: Output must be parseable for automated posting.

constraints.label: Automation
constraints.text: Findings feed an automated PR-comment step.

question: Which configuration fixes both problems?

options.text: Run with the -p/--print flag for non-interactive mode and use --output-format json (with a schema) to produce machine-parseable findings.
Set a made-up CLAUDE_HEADLESS=true variable and keep free-text output.
Redirect stdin from /dev/null and parse the prose with regex.
Add a --batch flag and read the console text.

correctReason: The -p/--print flag runs Claude Code non-interactively so CI does not hang, and --output-format json with a schema yields structured, machine-parseable findings for automated PR comments.

whyFail.B: That environment variable does not exist, and free-text output is still not machine-parseable.
whyFail.C: A stdin redirect is a workaround that does not match the documented flag, and regex on prose is brittle.
whyFail.D: There is no such --batch flag, and console text remains unstructured.
